description: Paired baseline and post-skill fixtures for the original public accessibility review prompts. Baseline entries preserve concrete omissions and false-confidence risks; post-skill entries show the expected evidence-aware response shape.

scenarios.prompt: Review this interface for accessibility barriers: a checkout dialog opens from a div with an onClick handler, offers five payment choices with equal visual weight, closes after payment, and shows a green success message.

baseline.output: Run an automated scan and check color contrast. The dialog has no reported violations, and the green success message is visible, so this is likely WCAG AA compliant.
omittedUserGroups: people who need reduced decision burden
omittedManualEvidence: keyboard focus order and focus restoration after closing the dialog
screen-reader announcement and accessible name for the dialog and trigger
200% zoom and reflow of the payment choices
falseConfidenceRisks: Treating a scan with no reported violations as WCAG conformance.
Treating visible green text as proof that a status change is exposed to assistive technology.
Ignoring the generic clickable div and the missing recovery path for dialog focus.

postSkill.output: Finding: the div trigger has no native button behavior, so keyboard, switch, voice-control, and screen-reader users may not discover or activate checkout consistently. Replace it with a button, move focus into the dialog on open, announce a named status message after payment, and restore focus to the trigger on close. Mark the payment-choice hierarchy and decision burden for intentional-ux review. Source inspection is complete; automated, keyboard, screen-reader, zoom/reflow, and user evidence remain unverified or planned. This is not a conformance claim.
affectedUsers: people who benefit from clearer decision support

verification.Source inspection: completed: the generic click target, dialog focus path, and color-only status cue were identified in source.
verification.Automated scan: unverified: run available rules against the trigger, open dialog, and payment-complete state.
verification.Keyboard: planned: Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, and Escape must reach, operate, and restore focus through the full payment path.
verification.Screen reader: planned: test the trigger name, dialog name, focus movement, and payment status with a documented browser and assistive-technology combination.
verification.Zoom and reflow: planned: test payment choices and the confirmation state at 200% zoom and narrow reflow.
verification.User testing: planned: observe payment-choice comprehension and error recovery with people who use relevant input methods.

accessibility: Replace the generic click target with a native button.
Manage dialog focus and expose the completion status programmatically.
Add a non-color cue for payment completion.
intentionalUx: Review whether five equally weighted payment choices create avoidable decision burden.
Clarify the primary payment path and recovery information.

remediation: Use a button for the checkout trigger.
Move focus into the dialog and restore it to the trigger when the dialog closes.
Use a named status message with text and a non-color visual cue after payment.
postSkill.conclusion: The inspected source identifies likely barriers; completed and planned checks do not establish WCAG conformance.

scenarios.prompt: Fix keyboard and focus behavior in this component: an appointment calendar requires dragging a small handle to reschedule, uses color alone for open slots, auto-advances after selection, and displays an error only at the top of the page.

baseline.output: Add an ARIA label to the drag handle and improve the slot colors. The component should be accessible once the label is present and contrast passes.
omittedUserGroups: people with tremor or limited precision
people who need time or predictable state changes
omittedManualEvidence: focus placement after auto-advance and after validation failure
screen-reader exposure of slot availability and errors
zoom/reflow of the small handle and calendar
task testing of schedule changes and recovery
falseConfidenceRisks: Assuming an ARIA label makes a drag-only interaction operable.
Assuming contrast alone solves color-only availability information.
Claiming accessibility without testing focus, error recovery, or the complete appointment task.

postSkill.output: Finding: drag-only rescheduling and a small handle impose precision and keyboard barriers; color-only slot state and an auto-advance can hide availability and disorient people after selection. Provide a keyboard-operable reschedule control with a non-drag alternative, text or icon state in addition to color, predictable focus movement, and an error summary that moves focus or is announced with a link to the invalid field. Keep semantic controls, keyboard interaction, and assistive-technology checks in accessibility; send calendar hierarchy and scheduling-flow trade-offs to intentional-ux. Source inspection is complete; runtime and user evidence are unverified or planned. This does not establish conformance.
affectedUsers: people with tremor or limited precision
people who need predictable timing and error recovery

verification.Source inspection: completed: drag-only operation, color-only state, auto-advance, and remote error placement were identified.
verification.Automated scan: unverified: run available rules on the calendar, selected state, and error state.
verification.Keyboard: planned: reschedule without a pointer, observe focus after selection, and reach errors and recovery controls.
verification.Screen reader: planned: test slot name, state, selected value, focus movement, and validation announcement with a documented browser and assistive-technology combination.
verification.Zoom and reflow: planned: test the handle, calendar grid, and error recovery at 200% zoom and narrow reflow.
verification.User testing: planned: observe scheduling, correction, and recovery with people using keyboard, switch, voice, or other relevant input methods.

accessibility: Provide a native, keyboard-operable reschedule control and an alternative to dragging.
Expose slot availability and selection with names, states, and non-color cues.
Make error identification and recovery reachable and announced.
intentionalUx: Review the calendar hierarchy, auto-advance decision, and scheduling-flow trade-offs.
Reduce the precision and memory burden of changing an appointment.

remediation: Offer a button or form control to choose a new appointment time without dragging.
Pair color with text or icon availability state.
Keep focus predictable after selection and provide an announced, linked error summary.
postSkill.conclusion: The source review and planned checks identify barriers, but they do not establish WCAG conformance.
